blog.heading: Research
blog.subtitle: Data notes from scoring real GitHub contribution at scale.
blog.notTranslated: This article hasn't been translated yet — showing the English original.

items.a: ghfind is a developer-scoring and discovery tool for GitHub. It rates any account from 0 to 100 for real contribution value and trustworthiness using a fully deterministic, open-source engine, then adds an optional AI-written roast, head-to-head battles, and a ranked developer directory.

The engine scores six weighted dimensions that sum to 100 — account maturity, original project quality, contribution quality, ecosystem impact, community influence, and activity authenticity — and subtracts penalties for red flags such as PR farming or star inflation. It runs only on public GitHub data.

items.q: Is the score deterministic and reproducible?
items.a: Yes. No LLM touches the number: the same inputs always produce the same score. The scoring core is open-sourced as the github-account-value skill (AGPL), so anyone can reproduce or audit a result. Only the optional roast text is LLM-written.

items.q: Can I use ghfind via API, CLI, or MCP?
items.a: Yes. There is a public REST API (see /openapi.json), official npm and PyPI packages plus a CLI, and a Model Context Protocol server at /mcp exposing score_user, scan_user, compare_users, get_leaderboard, and search_users tools. The score endpoint needs no authentication.

items.q: Does ghfind detect bots and AI-farmed accounts?
items.a: It flags the patterns that separate farmed activity from honest work — near-identical template PR titles, contributions concentrated on repos the account doesn't own, and suspicious star or follower inflation. In a dataset of 18,947 scored accounts, fewer than 1% tripped the strongest red flags — rare, but extreme when present.

No. Scores use public signals only, so private-organization contributions are invisible to the engine. An active member of private repos may be underrated. A low score is not a factual claim about a person — it describes an account's public footprint.

items.q: How do I remove my profile from ghfind?
items.a: Only accounts scoring 60+ are indexed and listed. To be removed from the board, use the removal link on the leaderboard footer or open an issue on the GitHub repository, and the profile will be delisted.

homeIntro.whatBody: ghfind is a developer-scoring and discovery tool for GitHub. It rates any account from 0 to 100 across six weighted dimensions — account maturity, original project quality, contribution quality, ecosystem impact, community influence, and activity authenticity — using a fully deterministic engine (the open-source github-account-value skill, AGPL). The same inputs always produce the same score; no LLM touches the number. On top of the score it adds an optional AI-written roast, developer-vs-developer battles, language and organization leaderboards, and README score badges.

steps.title: 1. Scan the public footprint
steps.body: Enter any GitHub username. The engine crawls the account's public data — repositories, pull requests, issues, stars, followers — and builds an evidence snapshot. No login and no permissions are required.

steps.title: 2. Score six deterministic dimensions
steps.body: The snapshot is scored across account maturity, original project quality, contribution quality, ecosystem impact, community influence, and activity authenticity, with penalties for PR-farming, star inflation, and template-spam patterns.

steps.title: 3. Get a score, tier, and roast
steps.body: You get a reproducible 0-100 score with a tier from Trash to God, a per-dimension breakdown showing exactly what holds the score down, and — only if you ask for it — a savage AI-written roast.

docs.metaTitle: ghfind API documentation — REST API, MCP server, SDKs
docs.metaDescription: Developer documentation for ghfind: REST endpoints for GitHub account scoring, the ghfind MCP server for AI agents, authentication, official npm and PyPI SDKs, and machine-readable specs (OpenAPI, llms.txt).
docs.heading: ghfind API documentation
docs.lead: ghfind scores any GitHub account 0-100 for real contribution value and trustworthiness with a deterministic, open-source engine. Everything on the site is also available programmatically: a public REST API, an MCP server for AI agents, and official SDKs. This page documents all of them.
docs.restIntro: Base URL: https://ghfind.com. Scoring reads need no authentication. Errors are structured JSON ({error, message, hint}), responses carry RateLimit-* headers, and list endpoints support limit/offset pagination.

endpoints.desc: Deterministic 0-100 score, tier, and six-dimension breakdown. Scores unseen accounts live; no auth, no LLM.
Full deterministic scan payload: raw metrics, top repositories, recent pull requests, red flags, sub-scores.
AI-written roast report (the only LLM endpoint; supports bring-your-own key).
Ranked developers by trending, score, heat, or progress; paginated via limit/offset.
Developer directory by language, organization, or contributed repository; paginated.
SVG score badge to embed in a README.

docs.mcpIntro: A Streamable HTTP Model Context Protocol server lives at https://ghfind.com/mcp (no auth, per-IP rate limited, read-only). Add it to Claude, Cursor, or any MCP client to score and compare GitHub accounts from inside the agent.

tools.name: score_user
tools.desc: 0-100 score, tier, and six-dimension breakdown for any GitHub login.

tools.name: scan_user
tools.desc: Full scan payload: metrics, top repos, recent PRs, red flags.

tools.name: compare_users
tools.desc: Deterministic head-to-head comparison with winner and score gap.

docs.authBody: Read endpoints are public. Write endpoints (POST /api/scan, POST /api/roast) accept a browser Turnstile verification or a Bearer API key. Agents should read the machine walkthrough at /auth.md.

about.metaDescription: ghfind scores any GitHub account 0-100 for real contribution value and trust with an open-source, deterministic engine, and helps you discover top developers.
about.heading: About ghfind
paragraphs: ghfind is a developer-scoring and discovery tool for GitHub. It rates any account from 0 to 100 for real contribution value and trustworthiness, using a fully deterministic engine that runs only on public GitHub data. The same inputs always produce the same score — no large language model is involved in the number itself — so results are reproducible and auditable rather than opaque.

The score is built from six weighted dimensions that sum to 100: account maturity, original project quality, contribution quality, ecosystem impact, community influence, and activity authenticity. On top of that factual core, ghfind adds an optional AI-written roast, developer-versus-developer battles, leaderboards by programming language, organization, and project, and README score badges you can embed on your own profile.

The scoring engine is open source under the AGPL license as the github-account-value skill, and the same code paths power the website, the official npm and PyPI packages, the command-line tool, and the Model Context Protocol server. ghfind exists to make GitHub contribution legible: to reward genuine open-source work, surface developers worth following, and make farmed or faked activity easy to spot by looking at patterns instead of raw counts.

contact.metaDescription: How to reach the ghfind team: report an issue, request removal from the leaderboard, or ask about the API, CLI, and MCP server.
contact.heading: Contact
paragraphs: ghfind is an open-source project. The fastest way to reach the team, report a problem, request removal from the leaderboard, or ask about the API, CLI, or MCP server is to open an issue on the public GitHub repository. Issues are read and triaged there, and keeping the conversation in the open helps other users who hit the same question.

For integration questions, the machine-readable API contract lives at /openapi.json, the agent-facing documentation index at /llms.txt, and the authentication walkthrough at /auth.md. If you need an elevated rate limit for high-volume server-to-server scanning, mention your use case in a GitHub issue.
contact.bizLead: For business inquiries, bulk licensing, or elevated API rate limits, email us directly:
contact.bizNote: (For open-source usage questions, please prefer a GitHub issue — public answers help other users too.)

privacy.metaDescription: How ghfind handles data: it scores only public GitHub data, does not sell personal data, and honors removal requests for indexed profiles.
privacy.heading: Privacy

sections.p: ghfind computes scores from public GitHub data only — the same information anyone can see on a profile and its public repositories, pull requests, and activity. It does not access private repositories, private contributions, email contents, or any data that is not already public on GitHub. Because of this, scores describe an account's public footprint and are not a factual claim about the person behind it.

For accounts that are scored, we cache the computed score, tier, and derived metrics so shared reports load quickly and the leaderboard stays fast. Only accounts scoring 60 or above are indexed and listed publicly; lower-scoring pages are reachable by direct link but kept out of search engines. We use privacy-respecting, aggregate analytics to understand traffic and do not sell personal data.

The optional roast feature can use your own model provider key. When you supply one, it stays in your browser and is relayed once through our server to call the model — it is never stored and never logged, and is discarded after the request completes.

If you would like your profile delisted, use the removal link on the leaderboard footer or open an issue on the GitHub repository, and we will remove it from the board and index. Because ghfind only reflects public GitHub data, updating or making your GitHub activity private will also change or remove what ghfind can see on the next scan.

methodology.metaTitle: Methodology — how ghfind scores GitHub accounts
methodology.metaDescription: How ghfind's deterministic engine scores GitHub accounts across six weighted dimensions, applies red-flag penalties, and stays fully reproducible without any LLM.
methodology.heading: How ghfind scores GitHub accounts
methodology.lead: ghfind's score is produced by a deterministic rubric, not a model. The engine reads public GitHub data, scores six dimensions that sum to 100, subtracts penalties for red flags, and returns the same number every time for the same inputs. The core is open source under the AGPL license, so any result can be reproduced and audited.
methodology.dimensionsIntro: Each dimension contributes to the 100-point base score. Together they reward sustained, original, widely-used open-source work over raw activity counts.

dimensionDetails.account_maturity: How established and consistent the account is over time — age, sustained activity, and a credibility curve that resists overnight inflation.
dimensionDetails.original_project_quality: The quality and reception of repositories the account actually owns and authored, not forks or trivial projects.
dimensionDetails.contribution_quality: The substance of contributions to other projects — merged, meaningful pull requests versus template spam.
dimensionDetails.ecosystem_impact: How much the account's work is depended on and maintained across the wider ecosystem.
dimensionDetails.community_influence: Genuine reach and standing in the community, normalized so raw follower or star counts cannot dominate.
dimensionDetails.activity_authenticity: Whether activity patterns look organic or engineered — the dimension that separates honest work from farming.

methodology.penaltiesBody: On top of the six dimensions, the engine subtracts penalties for patterns that inflate a profile without real value: near-identical template pull-request titles, contributions concentrated on repositories the account does not own, and star or follower inflation. Every individual event can look plausible; the penalties fire on the pattern, which is what makes farmed activity separable from honest work.
methodology.determinismBody: No large language model touches the score. The scoring core is the open-source github-account-value skill (AGPL), and the same code paths power the website, the npm and PyPI packages, the CLI, and the MCP server. Given the same public inputs, the score is identical across all of them, which is why it can be independently reproduced. The optional roast text is the only LLM-written part and never changes the number.
methodology.dataBody: Applied to a public dataset of 18,947 scored accounts — 3,444 with full raw-metric snapshots — faked or farmed contribution turned out to be rarer than the discourse suggests (under 1% of accounts tripped the strongest red flags) but far more extreme when it appeared. The full analysis is in the research section.

vs.metaDescription: {a} vs {b} — a head-to-head GitHub showdown. Who's more hardcore? See it on ghfind.com.
vs.heading: Developer duel
vs.pkSub: Drop two GitHub handles and see who's more hardcore
vs.pkInvalid: Enter two different valid GitHub handles
vs.summonHint: Summon them to the ring to start the fight
vs.bothMissing: Neither has been judged yet — summon them first, then duel.
vs.verdictGenerating: 🤖 The AI is cooking up a savage verdict…
vs.embedCopied: ✅ Copied — paste it into your README
vs.shareText: {a} vs {b} — who's more hardcore? Watch this GitHub duel on ghfind.com ⚔️
vs.trendingSub: The most-watched GitHub developer PKs — tap in for the savage verdict
vs.trendingEmpty: No battles on the board yet. Type "a vs b" on the home page to fire the first shot.
vs.verdictMissing: Hold your verdict — summon them to the ring first.
vs.verdictTie: Neck and neck — nobody would call this one a robbery.

verdictCrush.0: @{winner} steamrolls @{loser} by {gap} points — this isn't a duel, it's a spectator sport.
verdictCrush.1: @{winner} {winnerScore} vs @{loser} {loserScore}: a gap so wide the ref didn't bother with the whistle.
verdictCrush.2: @{loser} is still warming up while @{winner} leads by {gap} — just shake hands and concede.
verdictCrush.3: @{winner} pins @{loser} to the mat; the {gap}-point chasm has no bottom.
verdictCrush.4: No suspense here: @{winner} dominates @{loser} across the board by {gap} points.
verdictCrush.5: @{loser} lost before the expression caught up — @{winner} takes a {gap}-point blowout.
verdictCrush.6: @{winner}'s {winnerScore} makes @{loser}'s {loserScore} look like a sparring partner.
verdictCrush.7: A discouraging margin: @{winner} leads @{loser} by a full {gap} points.

verdictEdge.0: @{winner} narrowly edges @{loser} by {gap} — the win wasn't cheap.
verdictEdge.1: Neck and neck, but @{winner} still laughs last on a {gap}-point edge.
verdictEdge.2: @{winner} {winnerScore} vs @{loser} {loserScore}: victory by a single step.
verdictEdge.3: @{loser} almost flipped it, but came up {gap} points short of @{winner}.
verdictEdge.4: In a tight match, @{winner} pulls slightly ahead and banks {gap} points.
verdictEdge.5: @{winner} wins, but @{loser} made it sweat — just {gap} points apart.
verdictEdge.6: A half-length win: @{winner} clears @{loser} by {gap} points.
verdictEdge.7: @{winner} squeaks through; @{loser} was one kick away.

verdictEven.0: @{winner} and @{loser} are all but even — {gap} points is basically noise.
verdictEven.1: Dead heat: nobody's robbed here — only {gap} points separate them.
verdictEven.2: @{winner} {winnerScore}, @{loser} {loserScore} — a different mood and it flips.
verdictEven.3: Too close to call: @{winner} and @{loser} stand on the same line.
verdictEven.4: Clash of titans — a {gap}-point gap is nothing to write home about.
verdictEven.5: @{winner} leads by a hair, but @{loser} isn't fazed at all.
verdictEven.6: Split the difference — @{winner} just got {gap} points luckier.
verdictEven.7: Six of one, half a dozen of the other — @{winner} and @{loser} gained nothing on each other.
